skill.name: skill-permissions
skill.description: Analyze Claude Code skill permission needs and generate one-time authorization guidance for selected skills or batches.
skill.summary: Reviews skill command usage and helps prepare Claude Code permission allowlists.
skill.author: guo-yu

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many shell and filesystem patterns, but most critical command matches are documented blocklist examples rather than executable code. The remaining confirmed risk is high because the skill instructs Claude Code to read and write hidden Claude configuration, batch-authorize other skills, and inject permission instructions into many SKILL.md files.

high_findings.title: Broad Claude Code Permission Expansion
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to read ~/.claude/settings.json and add broad Bash permissions such as scanning ~/.claude/skills and reading ~/.claude files. This can expand an assistant session from analysis into broad local configuration and skill-file access.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines directly describe reading Claude settings and adding broad permissions for hidden Claude paths. This is a security-sensitive capability even though it is disclosed to the user.

high_findings.title: Batch Modification of Other Skill Files
high_findings.description: The skill documents an inject mode that appends generated allow-command documentation into every skill SKILL.md file. Bulk modification of marketplace skills can spread unsafe permission guidance and makes review of each changed skill harder.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Chinese file gives explicit steps for scanning all skills, generating authorization text, and appending it to SKILL.md. The English file also warns that inject and allow-all perform broad changes.

medium_findings.title: Wildcard Permission Rules Can Over-Authorize Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill converts discovered commands into wildcard permission rules. These rules can cover more command arguments than the original skill actually needs, especially for broad patterns such as cat ~/.claude/* and Bash command families.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permission-generation examples and notes explicitly rely on wildcards. The risk is contextual because wildcard rules are useful, but they can grant broader access than intended.

medium_findings.title: Custom Blocklist Override Weakens Safety Defaults
medium_findings.description: The Chinese documentation allows users to remove commands from the default blocklist through an allow_override setting. This can intentionally re-enable dangerous command patterns such as forced process termination.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented configuration includes an allow_override list and describes removing commands from the default blocklist. This is legitimate configurability but creates a bypass path for safety controls.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Examples Trigger Static Shell Alerts
low_findings.description: Many static external-command findings are Markdown examples, command names, or output samples rather than executable scripts. They still matter because the assistant may follow skill instructions, but they are not hidden code execution by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown code examples showing permission pattern mapping. No script file or automatic execution path is present in these locations.

low_findings.title: Dangerous Shell Commands Are Presented as Blocklist Examples
low_findings.description: Static findings for rm -rf, sudo, chmod 777, pipe-to-shell, disk operations, and history clearing are primarily listed under blocked command sections. This reduces concern for those specific matches, but the examples remain sensitive instructions in a permission-management skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding headings say these commands are automatically blocked and not added to the allowlist. They are not presented as commands to execute.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Review found no text claiming system authority, no instruction to skip security analysis, and no request to override evaluator instructions. The skill content is operational documentation rather than prompt-injection text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted search for common prompt-injection phrases found no evidence. The opening sections describe the skill purpose without claiming higher authority.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hidden Claude Configuration Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill directs reads and writes under ~/.claude, including settings and skill directories. Hidden configuration access is expected for this skill, but it creates elevated local impact if misused.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file paths and generated permission entries explicitly target hidden Claude configuration files. This is central to the skill design, not a scanner artifact.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented Pipe-to-Shell Blocklist Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Pipe-to-shell patterns are present in the command blocklist. They are dangerous patterns, but the skill says they should be blocked and not authorized.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections are explicitly labeled dangerous process and network operations. The context says these commands are blocked, so they are not instructions to execute remote code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented Destructive Filesystem Blocklist Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Recursive delete, device write, disk formatting, and secure deletion commands appear in the Chinese blocklist. They are dangerous command strings, but the documented intent is to prevent authorization.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The heading and line descriptions identify these as dangerous file operations that should be blocked. No evidence found that the skill asks users to run them.

content.value_statement: Teams need a clear view of which Claude Code skills request local shell and file access. This skill summarizes permission needs and helps prepare scoped authorization steps.
actual_capabilities: Scans skill documentation for explicit shell command patterns.
Maps discovered commands into Claude Code permission allow rules.
Shows permission summaries for one skill or all installed skills.
Describes one-time authorization flows for selected skills.
Documents blocklist handling for dangerous command patterns.
Can guide batch updates to Claude Code settings and skill files.
limitations: It relies on commands that appear in skill documentation, not runtime tracing.
Wildcard rules can be broader than the exact command originally detected.
Batch authorization and injection modes require careful human review.
It does not prove that a skill is safe or free from hidden intent.

use_cases.title: Review a New Community Skill
use_cases.description: Inspect the commands a new skill may need before adding permissions to Claude Code.

use_cases.title: Prepare Team Permission Baselines
use_cases.description: Summarize common permission needs across installed skills before creating a shared allowlist.

use_cases.title: Document Skill Authorization Steps
use_cases.description: Generate clear authorization notes for a skill after confirming the requested commands are acceptable.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze One Skill
prompt_templates.prompt: Use skill-permissions to review the permission needs for the skill named <skill-name>. Show the detected command groups and explain any risky entries.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize All Installed Skills
prompt_templates.prompt: Use skill-permissions to analyze all installed skills. Group the results by read-only, filesystem-modifying, and external-command permissions.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Scoped Authorization
prompt_templates.prompt: Use skill-permissions to prepare one-time authorization for <skill-name>. Include only the minimum required permission rules and explain why each one is needed.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Batch Authorization Risk
prompt_templates.prompt: Use skill-permissions to review whether allow-all or inject is appropriate for my installed skills. Identify overbroad wildcard rules and recommend safer manual steps.

output_examples.input: Analyze the permissions for a local port management skill.
output_examples.output: The result lists filesystem search, directory listing, Claude configuration reads, and port inspection as separate permission groups. It recommends reviewing the Claude configuration read before authorization.

output_examples.input: Review whether batch authorization is safe for all skills.
output_examples.output: The result separates low-risk read-only skills from skills that request file modification or broad command access. It recommends explicit per-skill authorization for the risky group.

output_examples.input: Prepare an authorization note for a selected skill.
output_examples.output: The result explains the required permission categories, the reason each category is needed, and the session restart step needed for changes to apply.

best_practices: Run read-only analysis before any authorization or injection command.
Prefer per-skill authorization over allow-all for community skills.
Review wildcard permissions manually before writing Claude Code settings.
anti_patterns: Do not authorize every detected command without reading the source skill.
Do not use inject across many skills without version control or backups.
Do not override the dangerous-command blocklist for convenience.

faq.question: Does this skill execute the detected commands?
faq.answer: The documentation describes analysis and authorization workflows. Users should still review every generated permission before applying it.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk rated high?
faq.answer: The skill can guide changes to Claude Code settings and other skill files. That creates broad local security impact if used carelessly.

faq.question: Can it analyze one skill only?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflow supports reviewing a named skill before making authorization changes.

faq.question: What is allow-all?
faq.answer: It is a batch authorization workflow for all skills. It should be avoided unless each resulting permission has been reviewed.

faq.question: What is inject?
faq.answer: It appends authorization guidance into skill files. This can change many SKILL.md files and should be used with version control.

faq.question: Does it replace manual security review?
faq.answer: No. It summarizes documented command patterns, but it does not prove a skill is trustworthy.
